sentences.base_language: My phone rang while I was at home.
sentences.target_language: رنّ هاتفي بينما كنت في البيت.
sentences.romanized: ranna hatifi baynama kuntu fi al-bayt.
sentences.literal: Rang my-phone while I-was in the-house.
sentences.grammarNote: رَنَّ (ranna, it rang) — Form I of ر-ن-ن (r-n-n, to ring/resound): a geminate root where the last two consonants are the same. هَاتِفِي (hātifī, my phone) — هَاتِف (active participle فَاعِل of ه-ت-ف, to call out) + suffix ي (my). بَينَمَا (baynamā, while) — a temporal conjunction: بَين (between/while) + مَا. It introduces a simultaneous past action.

sentences.base_language: It was my friend Layla calling.
sentences.grammarNote: كَانَت صَدِيقَتِي لَيلى تَتَّصِل (kānat ṣadīqatī Laylā tattaṣil, my friend Layla was calling) — كَانَ + present verb = past continuous. تَتَّصِل (tattaṣil, she is calling) — Form VIII of و-ص-ل (w-ṣ-l, to connect): اِتَّصَلَ = to make contact/call. صَدِيقَتِي (ṣadīqatī, my friend, feminine) — feminine form of صَدِيق: صَدِيقَة + suffix ي.

أَهلاً (ahlan, hello/welcome) — accusative verbal noun from أَهل (family/people), used as a frozen greeting. Literally 'you are among family.' الأَخبَار (al-akhbār, the news) — broken plural of خَبَر (khabar, news/information), pattern أَفعَال. From root خ-ب-ر (kh-b-r, to know/inform): خَبَرَ (to inform), خَبِير (expert/informed). مَا الأَخبَار؟ = what is the news? — a nominal question with no verb.

sentences.base_language: I have great news — I passed the exam!
sentences.target_language: عندي أخبار رائعة — نجحت في الامتحان!
sentences.literal: At-me news wonderful — I-succeeded in the-exam!
sentences.grammarNote: عِندي (indi) = I have (literally: at me). Arabic has no verb 'to have'. Instead, possession is expressed with عِند (at/with) + pronoun suffix: عِندي (I have), عِنده (he has), عِندها (she has). Another option is لَدَيَّ (ladayya), which is slightly more formal.

sentences.base_language: Really?! Congratulations, I'm so happy for you!
sentences.target_language: حقاً؟! مبروك، أنا سعيد جداً من أجلك!
sentences.romanized: haqqa?! mabruk, ana sa'id jiddan min ajlik!
sentences.literal: Really?! Blessed, I happy very for-your-sake!
sentences.grammarNote: حَقَّاً (ḥaqqan, really/truly) — indefinite accusative adverb from حَقّ (truth/right), root ح-ق-ق: a frozen adverb. مَبرُوك (mabrūk, congratulations/blessed) — passive participle مَفعُول Form I of ب-ر-ك (b-r-k, to bless): literally 'one who has been blessed.' مِن أَجلِكِ (min ajliki, for your sake, feminine) — a common purpose phrase: مِن أَجل + pronoun suffix. The feminine suffix كِ addresses Layla.

sentences.base_language: I worked so hard for this exam.
sentences.target_language: اجتهدت كثيراً من أجل هذا الامتحان.
sentences.romanized: ijtahadtu kathiran min ajl hadha al-imtihan.
sentences.grammarNote: اِجتَهَدتُ (ijtahadtu, I worked hard) — Form VIII of ج-ه-د (j-h-d, to strive/exert): اِجتَهَدَ = to make a great effort. The root ج-ه-د gives جِهَاد (striving/effort) and مُجتَهِد (diligent). كَثِيراً (kathīran, a lot) — accusative adverb modifying the verb. مِن أَجل (min ajl, for the sake of) — a purpose preposition.

أنتِ (anti) is the feminine form of 'you'. In Arabic, verbs also change for gender: تَستحقِّين (tastahiqqin) for a female subject vs. تَستحِقُّ (tastahiqq) for a male. Here Layla is female so the feminine form is used throughout.

sentences.base_language: We must celebrate! Are you free this evening?
sentences.target_language: يجب أن نحتفل! هل أنتِ فارغة هذا المساء؟
sentences.romanized: yajib an nahtafil! hal anti farigha hadha al-masa'?
sentences.literal: Must that we-celebrate! Q you (f.) free this the-evening?
sentences.grammarNote: يَجِب أَن نَحتَفِل (yajib an naḥtafil, we must celebrate) — يَجِب (it is necessary, Form I of و-ج-ب, to be obligatory) + أَن + subjunctive. نَحتَفِل — Form VIII of ح-ف-ل (ḥ-f-l, to celebrate): اِحتَفَلَ = to celebrate. فَارِغَة (fārigha, free/empty, feminine) — active participle Form I of ف-ر-غ (f-r-gh, to be empty): here meaning 'free/available.'

أَنَا فَارِغَة (ana fārigha, I am free, feminine) — Layla speaks about herself; the feminine adjective فَارِغَة reflects her gender. In Arabic, adjectives and participles used predicatively must agree with the subject in gender. بَعدَ السَّابِعَة (baʿd as-sābiʿa, after seven) — السَّابِعَة (the seventh) = seven o'clock; ordinal feminine for telling time.

sentences.base_language: Let's go to that new restaurant near the park.
sentences.target_language: لنذهب إلى ذلك المطعم الجديد بالقرب من الحديقة.
sentences.romanized: li-nadhhab ila dhalika al-mat'am al-jadid bil-qurb min al-hadiqa.
sentences.literal: Let-us-go to that the-restaurant the-new by-the-nearness from the-park.
sentences.grammarNote: لِنَذهَب (li-nadhhab, let's go) — cohortative: لِـ + first-person plural jussive. ذَلِكَ المَطعَم (dhālika al-maṭʿam, that restaurant) — ذَلِكَ (that, m.) + definite noun: the demonstrative precedes the noun and the noun carries الـ. المَطعَم الجَدِيد (al-maṭʿam al-jadīd, the new restaurant) — definite noun + definite adjective, both with الـ. بِالقُرب مِن (bil-qurb min, near) — a fixed locative phrase.

sentences.base_language: We ate a delicious meal and talked for hours.
sentences.grammarNote: أَكَلنَا (akulnā, we ate) — Form I past of أ-ك-ل (ʾ-k-l, to eat) + suffix نَا (we). وَجبَة لَذِيذَة (wajba ladhīdha, a delicious meal) — both indefinite: وَجبَة (meal, from root و-ج-ب, to be due/obligatory — a meal is 'obligatory nourishment') + لَذِيذَة (delicious, feminine, from ل-ذ-ذ). تَحَدَّثنَا (taḥaddathnā, we talked) — Form V of ح-د-ث + suffix نَا.

sentences.base_language: It was a wonderful evening and I felt so proud of her.
sentences.target_language: كانت أمسية رائعة وشعرت بفخر كبير بها.
sentences.romanized: kanat umsiyya ra'i'a wa-sha'artu bi-fakhr kabir biha.
sentences.literal: Was evening wonderful and-I-felt with-pride great with-her.
sentences.grammarNote: شَعَرتُ بِـ (sha'artu bi-) = I felt [something]. The preposition بِـ (bi-) after شَعَرَ turns the noun into the thing felt: شَعَرَ بِفَرَح (he felt joy), شَعَرَت بِفَخر (she felt pride). This بِـ is not optional — omitting it would change the meaning.
